text.title: linear-issue-tracking Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for linear-issue-tracking. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Configure each team with: key prefix (e.g., BAK), workflow states (Backlog/Todo/In Progress/In Review/Done/Canceled), Fibonacci estimate scale (0/1/2/3/5/8/13), and triage enabled.
rule.rationale: Team key prefixes make issue identifiers human-readable across multi-team workspaces (BAK-123 vs FE-456); Fibonacci scale prevents false precision in estimates.

rule.statement: Set cycle duration to 2 weeks, start day Monday, auto-archive enabled — cycles provide time-boxing without requiring manual sprint management.
rule.rationale: Linear cycles auto-advance and archive completed issues; manual sprint management adds overhead that Linear's opinionated workflow is designed to eliminate.

rule.statement: Required issue properties: Status, Priority (Urgent/High/Medium/Low/No Priority), Estimate (Fibonacci), Cycle, Project, Label, Assignee. Priority is an integer enum (Urgent=1, High=2, Medium=3, Low=4, NoPriority=0) in the API — not a string.
rule.rationale: Agents receiving natural-language priority must map explicitly to integers before calling the API; passing strings causes silent mutation failure.

rule.statement: Cycle scope must be frozen after Day 2 of the cycle; API-driven cycle assignment must target nextCycle or futureCycle only — never the active cycle.
rule.rationale: Mid-cycle additions disrupt velocity tracking and flow; agents that auto-add issues to active cycles create scope creep without human intent.

rule.statement: Cycle IDs rotate every cycle; always resolve the current cycle by querying team.activeCycle — never hardcode a cycle ID in agent configuration.
rule.rationale: Hardcoded cycle IDs break every 2 weeks when the cycle advances; dynamic resolution via activeCycle query is always correct.

rule.statement: Projects span multiple cycles and represent initiatives; use milestones within projects to mark key deliverables with target dates.
rule.rationale: Cycles are time-boxes; projects are outcome-scopes. Mixing them confuses velocity (cycle) with delivery (project completion).

rule.statement: Process triage in this order: valid? → urgent? → scoped? → ready for backlog. Invalid issues go to Closed/Duplicate immediately; unscoped issues get "Needs Refinement" label and stay in triage.
rule.rationale: Triage order prevents time spent scoping issues that should be closed; "Needs Refinement" creates a visible queue for refinement sessions without cluttering the backlog.

rule.statement: Triage is a human-only step; agents must not auto-close or auto-route issues from the triage inbox without a human checkpoint.
rule.rationale: Triage decisions require context that agents cannot infer from issue text alone (duplicate detection, organizational priority, business context).

rule.statement: Configure GitHub integration: branch format "{username}/bak-{issue_number}-{issue_title}", PR-opened → "In Review", PR-merged → "Done", commit pattern "Fixes BAK-\\d+".
rule.rationale: Consistent branch naming enables bidirectional traceability; fix commit pattern auto-links PRs to issues; auto-transitions eliminate manual status updates.

rule.statement: Store issue identifiers (e.g., BAK-123) in git branch names and PR titles to enable bidirectional traceability between Linear issues and GitHub PRs.
rule.rationale: Linear's GitHub integration matches PRs to issues by identifier in branch name or PR title; without this, no automatic status transitions occur.

rule.statement: Create four standard views: my_work (assignee:me, group by priority), team_board (team filter, group by status, show estimates), bugs_triage (label:bug, status:triage, sort by created date), release_scope (project filter, status:-canceled).
rule.rationale: These four views cover the four daily PM workflows: individual task focus, team coordination, bug queue, and release tracking — without proliferating context-specific views.

rule.statement: Use issueSearch with server-side filters rather than fetching issues and filtering client-side — server-side filtering is 10x faster on large workspaces.
rule.rationale: Client-side filtering on 5000+ issues requires fetching all pages then discarding most results; server-side filters push this work to Linear's query engine.

rule.statement: Convert SDD implementation-plan.md TASK_ blocks to Linear issues: map task complexity (Low → NoPriority=0, Medium → Medium=3, High → High=2, Critical → Urgent=1); validate acceptance criteria non-empty before calling issueCreate mutation.
rule.rationale: Linear's issueCreate mutation does not validate acceptance criteria completeness; the agent must enforce this before calling the API, otherwise issues enter the backlog without enough information for developers.

rule.statement: Implement cursor-based pagination for all issues queries; Linear GraphQL returns max 250 items per page by default. Pass after: cursor argument with endCursor from previous response until hasNextPage is false.
rule.rationale: Agents that fetch only the first page silently miss issues beyond the page size; partial results produce incorrect velocity counts and incomplete blockers lists.

rule.statement: Always resolve the active cycle by querying team.activeCycle at runtime; never hardcode a cycle ID in agent configuration or prompts.
rule.rationale: Cycle IDs rotate every 2 weeks; hardcoded IDs break silently after the cycle advances, causing all cycle-assignment mutations to fail without an obvious error.

rule.statement: Verify Linear webhook HMAC signature on every incoming request; reject requests without valid signatures — an agent endpoint without HMAC validation is an injection vector.
rule.rationale: Linear sends webhooks with an X-Linear-Signature header containing an HMAC-SHA256 signature; without validation any caller can inject fake events into the agent pipeline.
